characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: The heroine, the Bennet family's second daughter. She is intelligent and independent-minded, but has a tendency to jump to conclusions and continuously judge people by her first impressions of them. Belligerent Sexual Tension: With Mr Darcy, except that it's one-sided. He's charmed, but she reallydoesn'tlike him, and doesn't begin to warm to him until he improves his own behavior. Birds of a Feather:With Mr Darcy, though she learns it only later.With Charlotte, in the platonic version; though Elizabeth loves her sister Jane dearly, she and Charlotte have much more similar personalities in most respects. With Mr Darcy, though she learns it only later. With Charlotte, in the platonic version; though Elizabeth loves her sister Jane dearly, she and Charlotte have much more similar personalities in most respects.

characters.name: Miss Elizabeth Bennet
characters.description: The heroine, the Bennet family's second daughter. She is intelligent and independent-minded, but has a tendency to jump to conclusions and continuously judge people by her first impressions of them. Belligerent Sexual Tension: With Mr Darcy, except that it's one-sided. He's charmed, but she reallydoesn'tlike him, and doesn't begin to warm to him until he improves his own behavior. Birds of a Feather:With Mr Darcy, though she learns it only later.With Charlotte, in the platonic version; though Elizabeth loves her sister Jane dearly, she and Charlotte have much more similar personalities in most respects. With Mr Darcy, though she learns it only later. With Charlotte, in the platonic version; though Elizabeth loves her sister Jane dearly, she and Charlotte have much more similar personalities in most respects.

characters.name: Mr Bennet
characters.description: The Bennet patriarch, an indolent gentleman with a rather more modest income than Mr Darcy. Of his daughters, he is closest to Elizabeth. Bookworm: Often found in his library, usually in retreat from the silliness of his wife and younger daughters. Brilliant, but Lazy: He's an intelligent man who loves to read, but he hates writing letters or conceding to societal demands (like calling on new neighbor Bingley). And he certainly doesn't put much effort at all into parenting his three youngest daughters. Cool Old Guy: His biting wit and kinship with Elizabeth makes him a very likable character, as does his enjoyment of the silliness of others. Deconstructed Character Archetype: If one gathered all the contemporary wisdom of what a Regency era country squire was supposed to be (educated, witty, living on his estate, unemotional, uninvolved in his daughters’ education, and unwilling to get his hands dirty with actual work) and distilled it into a single character, one would get something very much like Mr Bennet. Unfortunately.

characters.name: Mrs Bennet
characters.description: The Bennet matriarch, a dramatic woman who is obsessed with marrying her five daughters off to rich men, and sees the arrival of Darcy and the Bingleys as an opportunity. Amazingly Embarrassing Parents: Oh yes. She's simpering when talking to someone she wants to impress (like Bingley), barely conceals her hostility towards Darcy; worst of all, she loudly and indiscreetly talks about her daughters' marriage prospects in public. She's also a bit of a hypocrite and has a tendency towardsSelf-Serving Memory. Brainless Beauty: She was apparently pretty and silly in her youth. Mr Bennet married her for the "beauty" part, then learned the hard way that beauty fades with age, leaving the bookish and intelligent man with an ill-suited wife. However, the text suggests Mrs Bennet is still a good-looking woman for her age. Deconstructed Trope: If one gathered all the contemporary wisdom of what a Regency era country squire’s wife was supposed to be (uneducated and yet wholly responsible for her daughters’ education, blissfully ignorant of the world outside her circle, fixated on getting her daughters married off no matter to whom, interested only in traditionally female subjects like fashion, housekeeping, and gossip, not overly troubled by logic or reason) and distilled it into a single character, one would get something very much like Mrs Bennet. Unfortunately. The Ditz: She's flighty, overreactive, easy prey to Mr Bennet's taunts, and doesn't comprehend how her behavior is embarrassing to her family.

characters.name: Miss Jane Bennet
characters.description: The eldest Bennet daughter, a sweet and gentle beauty. Beauty Equals Goodness: In her case, it does. She's the prettiest and the nicest of the Bennet girls, as well as the nicest character in the book as a whole. Beta Couple: With Bingley; they fall in love almost at once and the complications in their relationship all come from the outside. Birds of a Feather: Jane Bennet and Bingley hit it off immediately when they meet. They are both sweet, kind-hearted and uncomplicated. Cool Big Sis: To Elizabeth. She tries to be this to the other girls too; they just don't give her credit for it.

characters.name: Miss Lydia Bennet
characters.description: The youngest of the Bennet sisters. She is spoiled and impulsive. All Take and No Give: Her relationship with everyone. When she "treats" Lizzy and Jane to lunch at an inn, she follows up by saying they'll have to lend her money because she's spent hers already. Annoying Younger Sibling: Elizabeth and Jane are both constantly trying to check her wild behavior to little effect. Attention Whore: Her favourites among the officers change constantly depending on who flatters her the best. Awful Wedded Life: Austen details how her marriage to Wickham gradually sours as he very soon loses whatever small affection he had for her; while her devotion to him lasts a bit longer, their life together is fraught as they're constantly having to rely on the charity of her sisters and never have a stable home.

characters.name: Miss Mary Bennet
characters.description: The middle Bennet sister. To compensate for being considered the plainest of the five, she tries more intellectual pursuits, and is prone to acting preachy as a result. Attention Whore: In a different fashion from Lydia. Mary takes every opportunity to try to show herself off as intelligent and moral, and also jumps at the chance to perform musically whether anyone wants her to or not. Bookworm: She's always seen reading, but she doesn't appear to think much about what she reads. She just wants to have the appearance of someone intelligent and deep. The Bore: Her recitations of what she's read are usually found to be tiresome by the rest of her family. Bratty Teenage Daughter: Mary is one because of her pompous moralizing and general self-involved attitude; she works too hard for accomplishments and praise because she's the least attractive of the five girls, and receives less attention than her prettier sisters. Mary does get better over time since she becomes her mother's companion once her sisters are married off, so she's forced to socialize more; what's more, without her sisters to be compared to she's no longer reduced to "the plain daughter," which makes her feel better about herself.

characters.name: Miss Catherine "Kitty" Bennet
characters.description: Bratty Teenage Daughter: Kitty tends to follow in Lydia's footsteps and as such is a milder version of her. Kitty does improve over time due to the considerably-better influence of her two oldest sisters, once Lydia is out of the picture (and her parents strictly forbid Kitty from answering her invitations). Middle Child Syndrome: Kitty catches the worst of this. She's overshadowed by Jane, Elizabeth and Lydia, and she's ignored by everyone as a result. She spends most of the book trailing in Lydia's wake until Lydia goes to Brighton. Only Known By Her Nickname: Her name's actually Catherine, but everyone calls her Kitty. Satellite Character: Spends most of the book hovering around Lydia. Took a Level in Kindness:Word of Godsays that she improved her manners after being separated from her obnoxious sister and companion Lydia. She eventually improved them enough toattract a country gentleman for a husband.invoked

characters.name: Mr William Collins
characters.description: Mr Bennet's nearest male relative, who is to inherit Longbourn after his passing. In the meantime, he has become a clergyman under the patronship of Lady Catherine de Bourgh. Babies Ever After: The last letter that Mr Bennet is shown receiving from him in the story mentions that he is expecting his first child. Birds of a Feather: He thinks his marriage is this, commenting to Elizabeth that he and Charlotte "seem to have been designed for one another." In reality, Charlotte just agrees with everything he says because it keeps him from bothering her. The Bore: He prattles on endlessly (usually about Lady Catherine), leading the Bennets to hide from him as best they can while he's a houseguest. His wife deals with it by encouraging him to do all sorts of healthy, worthwhile activities - reading, walking to Rosings, tending to his garden - which conveniently limits their interaction as much as possible. The Ditz: He has no idea, first of all, that Mr Bennet is mocking him ruthlessly, nor that his wife only married him out of practicality.

characters.name: Mr Edward Gardiner
characters.description: The Bennet sisters' maternal uncle, who is a tradesman. Cool Uncle: He's very affectionate toward his nieces, especially Elizabeth and Jane, and he's much more levelheaded than his sisters. Happily Married: He and his wife have a loving relationship. They have several small children. They serve as an example of a good marriage of affection to the Bennet sisters. Self-Made Man: He's in trade and lives in sight of his warehouses, so the Bingley sisters look down on him, but he's very successful and well-off; he can afford servants and vacations.

characters.name: Mrs Gardiner
characters.description: The Bennet sisters' maternal aunt. Amazingly Embarrassing Parents: Inverted. She and her husband are the only members of Elizabeth's family she never blushes over.Lampshadedby Elizabeth herself when they meet Darcy. Good Parents: To her and her husband's several children, who are good and well-behaved. No Name Given: Her first initial is M. Other than that, we don't know. The 1995 miniseries names her "Madeline," but that is very unlikely for the era; more likely options are Mary, Margaret, etc. (Most likely it's Mary and her niece Mary was named after her.) Parental Substitute: To Elizabeth. She's got an excellent head on her shoulders, far better than Mrs Bennet, and both respects and advises her niece.

characters.name: Mr Charles Bingley
characters.description: An amiable young man whose family fortune is fairly new. He leases the estate Netherfield and gets along with Jane. Birds of a Feather: With Jane, and it's why they fall in love. Mr Bennet jokes that they're both so obliging that nothing will ever be agreed on and so generous that servants will always cheat them. Extreme Doormat: An unusually cheerful (and male) version, but he yields easily to persuasion. Darcy even points this out as a fault, saying that if Bingley made every preparation to go on a journey and had one foot in the saddle, just a word from a friend could stop him. Heterosexual Life-Partners: With Darcy. Love at First Sight: With Jane.

characters.name: Miss Caroline Bingley
characters.description: Bingley's unmarried sisternotehe has a married one, Louisa, but adaptations tend toCompositethe two as Louisa is very much a background character and pretty much vanishes during the second half of the story. She desires to marry Mr Darcy and is thus jealous of his connection to Elizabeth. Accidental Truth: She "warns" Lizzy to stay away from Mr Wickham, having no idea just how right she is. Bitch in Sheep's Clothing: Very friendly and attentive to Jane... until she learns of Jane's "low" connections. Clingy Jealous Girl: Frequently disparages Lizzy and her family in front of Darcy. Compliment Fishing: Is prone to this around Mr. Darcy, usually while also aiming Sugary Malice at her romantic rival Elizabeth, whom Darcy clearly favours over her.

characters.name: Mr Fitzwilliam Darcy
characters.description: The romantic lead, anextremelyproud and wealthy gentleman who owns a lavish estate, Pemberley, in Derbyshire. He is a close friend of Mr Bingley and regards his association with the Bennets with suspicion, even as he first grows intrigued by and then is attracted to Elizabeth. Anguished Declaration of Love: Darcy's first proposal is more accurately an Anguished Declaration Of Why I Love You Even Though You And Your Family Are Clearly Beneath Me. As usually with this trope, it fails to impress Elizabeth. His second proposal is a much more nicely wordedLove Confession. Belligerent Sexual Tension: With Elizabeth Bennet...except that it's one-sided on his end. He's charmed by her lively wit and her unabashed willingness to tease him, but she reallydoesn'tlike him, and doesn't begin to warm to him until he improves his own behavior. Big Brother Instinct: He's very protective of his little sister Georgiana. He also tries to protect his close friend Bingley, who regularly appeals to him for advice and whom he does his best to shield from the possible ill consequences of his overly pliant personality. Big Damn Heroes: Though it's entirely from a social standpoint, Darcy's arranging the marriage between Lydia and Wickham saves the Bennets from theirDarkest Hour. Particularly in the 1995 serial, where we see him personally find the fugitives and isrightbehind Wickham in the church—with an expression as though he's prepared to pummel him right there and then if he hesitates in repeating the vows.

characters.name: Lady Catherine de Bourgh
characters.description: Darcy's maternal aunt, an imperious and snobby middle-aged woman. She, like Darcy's mother, is the daughter of an earl, and wants Darcy to keep the wealth in the family. Bullying a Dragon: Thinks Elizabeth is doing this by being in love with Darcy, but when she tries aHannibal Lectureon Elizabeth, she finds herselfquite spectacularly out of her depth. Grande Dame: Wealthy, imperious, and entirely unprepared for anyone crossing her will. Hair-Trigger Temper: A hint of resistance will make her come down hard. Hannibal Lecture: Gives Elizabeth a particularly nasty one but soongets her ass handed to her.

characters.name: Miss Georgiana Darcy
characters.description: Darcy's beloved younger sister, an accomplished yet shy lady. Big Brother Worship: The reason she's so eager to meet Elizabeth is because she takes Darcy's opinion as gospel. Elegant Classical Musician: She is an accomplished pianist. Hair of Gold, Heart of Gold: In the 1995 miniseries and the 2005 adaptation. Hero-Worshipper: Sheadoresher brother and adores Elizabeth even before meeting her because Darcy likes her.

characters.name: Colonel Fitzwilliam
characters.description: Darcy's cousin, who raised Georgiana alongside him. Locked Out of the Loop: As he explains to Elizabeth, Fitzwilliam is privy to Darcy's efforts in preventing Bingley's prospective courtship of some unsuitable Hertfordshire lady, but is also completely ignorant that the lady in question just so happens to be Elizabeth's sister. Nice Guy: Elizabeth finds the Colonel to be an amiable sort, and he is probably Darcy's closest friend besides Bingley. No Name Given: Unlike Darcy, "Fitzwilliam" is the Colonel's last name, and we never find out what his first name may be.

characters.name: Mr George Wickham
characters.description: A charming militia officer who has a bitter past with Mr Darcy. Arch-Enemy: To Mr. Darcy. Darcy despises him for his pursuit of Georgiana and is so openly hostile to him that Elizabeth wrongly assumes that he irrationally hates Wickham. Bitch in Sheep's Clothing: As Elizabeth says later, he has all theappearanceof goodness, not goodness itself. He easily charms Hertfordshire society with his pleasant and charming manners, but at the core he's a selfish bastard. Brilliant, but Lazy: The elder Mr. Darcy was so fond of young Wickham that he paid for him to receive a good education, including attending Cambridge. Combined with Wickham's carefully-cultivated charm this would normally give him ahugeeducational advantage in seeking a respectable career. But since he would rather marry moneyand/orwin it at the gambling tables he puts no effort into this. Even joining the Militia was avoidance of a real career, as the Militia was not sent to the Continent to fight in the Napoleonic Wars and they spent most of their time in pleasant locales in England with plenty of opportunity to attend assemblies and, of course, engage in gambling. The Casanova: Among the complaints about him is that he seduces (or tries to) every young woman in Hertfordshire.

characters.name: Miss Charlotte Lucas
characters.description: Elizabeth's best friend. Babies Ever After: Is soon pregnant following her marriage to Mr Collins. First-Name Basis: With Elizabeth. Notable because this is an era where you call everyone but your family or your absolute closest friends "Miss X", "Mrs Y" or "Mr Z". Not only does she call Elizabeth by her first name, she calls her by the diminutive "Eliza", showing that they're onveryclose terms and are practically family. She also calls Jane, the sister Elizabeth is closest with, by her first name. Foil: To Elizabeth. Although they're great friends, Elizabeth is determined toMarry for Love, and Charlotte is determined toMarry For Eminently Practical Reasons. Happily Married: Played with. She is happyto bemarried and finds her situation quite comfortable, but she quietly arranges her married life to have as little actual contact with Mr Collins as possible. Interestingly Mr Collins seems every bit as happy with this arrangement as she is.
